result:
The old woman at the bus stop was eating an orange. I noticed this because she peeled it in one continuous spiral, the rind unfurling like a party ribbon between her weathered fingers. It was 7:43 AM on a Tuesday, and this small act of patience felt revolutionary.

I've been thinking lately about how we've forgotten slowness. Not the frustrating kind – waiting in line at the DMV or buffering videos – but the deliberate kind. The kind that woman had, turning that orange in her palm like she was unwinding time itself.

My grandfather used to sharpen pencils with a pocket knife. Each curl of wood would fall to his workshop floor, and he'd say the best things couldn't be rushed. "You can't hurry a tomato," he'd tell me, which made no sense when I was eight but makes perfect sense now that I'm thirty-five and trying to grow them on my apartment balcony. They're still green, stubbornly so, despite my daily inspections and whispered encouragements.

The bus came and went. The woman didn't get on. She just sat there, separating each segment of orange with surgical precision, placing them on a napkin beside her like she was arranging tarot cards. I wanted to ask her what fortune she was reading in that citrus constellation, but strangers don't talk at bus stops anymore. We look at our phones instead, scrolling through other people's curated moments while our own uncurated ones slip by unnoticed.

There's a theory that time moves faster as you age because each year becomes a smaller percentage of your total life experience. When you're five, a year is twenty percent of everything you've known. When you're fifty, it's just two percent. But I think it's simpler than that. I think we just stop paying attention to orange peels.

My daughter called yesterday. She's in college now, studying something I don't quite understand involving computers and human behavior. She said she was learning about attention economics, how our focus has become currency in the digital age. "They're literally buying and selling our ability to concentrate, Dad," she said, and I could hear her outrage through the phone. I wanted to tell her about the woman with the orange, but I didn't know how to make it relevant to her world of algorithms and engagement metrics.

When did relevance become so important anyway? Not everything needs to connect to everything else. Sometimes an orange is just an orange, and watching someone peel one is just a moment of unexpected grace on an ordinary Tuesday morning.

I've started walking to work instead of driving. It takes forty minutes longer, but I've discovered things: a house that's painted the exact color of a cloudless sky, a tree whose roots have lifted the sidewalk into a gentle wave, a man who sits on his porch every morning at 8:15 and waves at everyone who passes. His name is Gabriel. I know this because last week I stopped waving back and started saying hello.

Gabriel told me he retired from the post office three years ago. "Delivered mail for thirty-seven years," he said. "Watched kids grow up, get married, have kids of their own. Watched the letters get fewer and the packages get bigger. People don't write anymore. They order things instead."

He's right, of course. My mailbox is mostly Amazon packages now, things I convinced myself I needed at 2 AM but can barely remember ordering. But last month, I wrote my daughter a letter. An actual letter, with a stamp and everything. She texted me a photo of herself holding it, looking confused but smiling. "Who writes letters?" her caption said. Fathers, I wanted to reply. Fathers who are trying to remember what their handwriting looks like.

The woman finished her orange. She folded the napkin around the peel, tucked it into her purse, and stood up. Another bus was approaching. This time, she got on, but not before turning to look at me. She smiled, and I realized she knew I'd been watching her the entire time. There was something in that smile – not judgment, but recognition. Like she was passing me a secret.

Since then, I've been practicing slowness. I make coffee in a French press instead of the instant machine. I read poetry even though I don't always understand it. I sit in my daughter's old room sometimes and remember when she was seven and insisted on painting one wall purple. It's still purple. Some things shouldn't be painted over.

Yesterday, I bought a bag of oranges. This morning, I sat at that same bus stop and peeled one, trying to keep the spiral intact. I failed. The rind broke three times, and juice ran down my wrist. But an elderly man walking his dog stopped and said, "I haven't seen anyone peel an orange like that in years. My mother used to hang the peels from the Christmas tree."

We talked for ten minutes about mothers and oranges and Christmas trees, and when he left, I realized this is what we've lost – not just slowness, but the possibility of connection that lives inside it. When we rush, we become islands. When we slow down, we become bridges.

The tomatoes on my balcony are finally starting to blush. My grandfather was right. You can't hurry them. You can't hurry anything worth having.

I think I'll write my daughter another letter tonight. I'll tell her about the woman with the orange, about Gabriel, about the way morning light catches the purple wall of her old room. Maybe she'll understand, maybe she won't.

But she'll have to slow down to read it.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
